climate: Hot and humid with frequent rain
essential_items: Quick-dry towel, padlock for hostel lockers, flip flops and one pair of walking shoes, rain poncho or packable jacket, reusable water bottle with filter (Sawyer Squeeze or LifeStraw), power adapter (Type A/C), portable charger 20000mAh, first aid kit with oral rehydration salts, anti-diarrheal medication, and insect repellent with DEET
clothing_strategy: 2-3 lightweight shirts (synthetic or linen), 2 pairs of quick-dry pants or shorts, 1 lightweight long pants for temple visits, 1 light layer for air-conditioned buses and restaurants, 7 pairs of underwear (merino wool dries fast and resists odor). Pack light — laundry services are cheap and ubiquitous (often $1-2/kg)
tech_and_documents: Scanned copies of passport and travel insurance in cloud storage, offline maps downloaded (Maps.me or Google Maps), local SIM card purchased at airport, emergency contact list stored offline, travel insurance with medical evacuation coverage — do not skip this
common_mistakes: Packing too many clothes (buy anything you need there for less), bringing full-size toiletries (buy locally), not accounting for weight of souvenirs on return, booking accommodation too far in advance (flexibility has value), not carrying small bills for street food and tuk-tuks
pro_tips: Roll clothes instead of folding to save space. Use packing cubes. Wear your heaviest items on travel days. Compression bags for bulky items like a down jacket. Leave space for inevitable purchases. A small day pack that folds into itself is invaluable for city days.

climate: Cold mountain with snow and ice
essential_items: Base layers (merino wool or synthetic — no cotton), mid-layer fleece or down, waterproof ski jacket and pants (can rent if not owning), ski socks (3-4 pairs of proper wool ski socks — not regular socks), balaclava or neck gaiter, ski gloves or mittens with liner, goggles, sunscreen SPF 50+ (UV is intense at altitude), lip balm with SPF, hand warmers
clothing_strategy: Layering system is everything: moisture-wicking base, insulating mid-layer, waterproof shell. Après-ski clothes: comfortable casual layers for evenings. Avoid jeans on the mountain — they are cold and dangerous when wet. Merino wool sweaters work for both mountain and resort town evenings.
tech_and_documents: Lift ticket and rental confirmations printed or saved offline, travel insurance with ski coverage (medical and equipment), emergency mountain rescue information for the specific resort, offline navigation if skiing in backcountry or unfamiliar terrain
common_mistakes: Renting poor-quality boots from resort shops — your own boots make an enormous performance difference. Not applying sunscreen (mountain sun at altitude causes serious burns even on cloudy days). Skipping helmet. Wearing too many layers and overheating, which leads to sweating and then chilling.
pro_tips: Rent skis locally, but bring your own boots if you ski frequently. Boot dryers are available at most ski accommodations — use them nightly. Pack a small belt bag for lift ticket, phone, snacks, and sunscreen on the mountain. Identify avalanche conditions and carry safety equipment if venturing off-piste.

essential_items: 2-3 business suits or equivalent (dress for the most formal meeting you will have), business casual options for meals and networking, comfortable dress shoes (broken in — not new), one pair of casual shoes for evenings, wrinkle-resistant fabrics, neutral colors that mix and match, a compact steamer or wrinkle-release spray
clothing_strategy: The 3-suit method: one dark suit, one medium suit, mix-and-match shirts and ties or blouses. Pack more formal than you think you need — underdressing in business is harder to recover from than overdressing. A navy suit is the most versatile. Black shoes match more than brown in business contexts.
tech_and_documents: Laptop and charging cables, international power adapter, noise-canceling headphones for flights, business cards, all meeting materials downloaded offline in case of poor wifi, travel loyalty program membership numbers ready, hotel loyalty membership for upgrades and late checkout
common_mistakes: Checking a bag on short business trips (time lost at baggage claim adds up). Not confirming visa requirements for international business (tourist visas may not cover business activities). Booking middle seats. Not using airline or hotel status to request upgrades.
pro_tips: Pack your suit jacket in a dry-cleaning bag to prevent wrinkling. Roll ties instead of folding. Use hotel dry cleaning for essentials arriving wrinkled. Keep essentials in your personal item in case your carry-on is gate-checked. A portable WiFi hotspot is worth it for unreliable hotel connections.

essential_items: Reef-safe sunscreen in generous quantity (SPF 50+, reapply every 2 hours), rash guard for extended water time, beach bag, reusable water bottle, after-sun lotion with aloe vera, light prescription sunglasses with UV protection, waterproof phone pouch, dry bag for beach electronics, light sandals and one pair of closed-toe shoes for exploring
clothing_strategy: Mix-and-match swimwear (2-3 swimsuits so one is always dry), lightweight linen or cotton cover-ups and shirts, one nicer outfit for restaurants, casual sundresses or shorts for town. Most items can be rinsed in sink and dry overnight in humid tropical air. One lightweight layer for air-conditioned restaurants and transfers.
tech_and_documents: All documents and hotel confirmations in waterproof bag or protected on phone, travel insurance with dive coverage if scuba diving, offline maps of the island (cell coverage can be poor on small islands), emergency numbers for local medical facilities
common_mistakes: Bringing valuables to the beach unnecessarily. Applying sunscreen after sun exposure is already under way. Not drinking enough water in heat and humidity. Booking every day in advance — island time is flexible and opportunities for day trips arise unexpectedly.
pro_tips: Golden hour (early morning and late afternoon) offers beautiful light for photography and is also when UV is least intense. Rent a scooter or bicycle to explore beyond tourist areas. Eat where local fishermen eat — the fish will be fresher and cheaper. Learn 5-10 words in the local language.

essential_items: Tent appropriate for conditions, sleeping bag rated for nighttime temps (go 10°F lower than expected minimum), sleeping pad (insulation from ground matters more than comfort), lightweight stove and fuel canister, water filter or purification tablets, headlamp with spare batteries, navigation tools (paper map and compass plus GPS), first aid kit with blister care and SAM splint, bear canister or hang bag in bear country
clothing_strategy: Layering system: moisture-wicking base (no cotton — it kills when wet), fleece mid-layer, waterproof rain shell, insulated jacket for camp evenings. Hiking boots or trail runners broken in before the trip. Wool socks with a thin liner to prevent blisters. One camp outfit kept dry and clean for sleeping.
tech_and_documents: Downloaded offline topo maps (AllTrails, Gaia GPS), park permit confirmations, emergency contact card left with someone at home, weather forecast for the specific area, knowledge of Leave No Trace principles
common_mistakes: Underestimating night temperatures (mountains drop fast after sunset). Boots that have not been broken in on a long trip. Insufficient calories — you burn significantly more hiking than daily life. Not treating water even from seemingly pristine streams.
pro_tips: Test all gear before leaving home. The 10 Essentials: navigation, sun protection, insulation, illumination, first aid, fire, repair tools, nutrition, hydration, emergency shelter. Pack out all trash including biodegradable items. Campfire rings should leave no trace.

essential_items: Comfortable walking shoes rated for 15,000+ steps per day (break them in first), small crossbody bag or anti-theft backpack for city security, reusable tote bag for museum shops and markets, compact umbrella, portable phone charger, notebook or journal for museum context notes, city public transit card if pre-purchasable
clothing_strategy: 3-4 days of smart casual outfits that look good in photos but can sustain a full day of walking. Avoid overly tourist-looking clothing — it marks you as a target. One nicer outfit for upscale restaurants or operas. Comfortable, attractive walking shoes are the most important investment — this is not the trip for new shoes.
tech_and_documents: Museum reservations downloaded and accessible offline (many major museums require timed entry now), transit apps installed, translation app with offline language packs, audio tour apps for major sites (Rick Steves, Google Arts and Culture), restaurant reservations confirmed in advance for popular destinations
common_mistakes: Not booking major museum and attraction tickets in advance — sold out is increasingly common at top sites. Carrying large backpack that slows entry through security. Overscheduling each day without buffer time for serendipitous discoveries. Only eating near tourist attractions.
pro_tips: Allocate mornings to major sights (smaller crowds), afternoons to neighborhoods and walking, evenings to food and neighborhoods. One unscheduled afternoon produces unexpected highlights. Museum fatigue is real — limit to 2-3 per day. Ask hotel concierge for non-tourist restaurant recommendations.
